transcript.sec: Rooting the next 100 years in founding values

turns.en: Well, I'm trying to synthesize it, and now with AI I'm more convinced I'll be able to synthesize all these conversations into something that actually makes sense. But to me, what's so important is actually to root the next 100 years in the values that I think have animated the founding of the country: freedom, equality, justice, life, liberty, pursuit of happiness. And take those and overlay them on different parts of our society. In education, what does freedom, equality, and justice actually look like in practice? What does that actually mean? The education system we have today was created 100 years ago for the industrial era in America. Everything from the school bell to everyone learning how to do individualized things and getting A, B, C grades—all of those things were really oriented around a career path that's not common in America today. If we had to rethink our education system aligned with our values but with an eye to what young people will be doing in the future, I think we would design education completely differently for the next 100 years. There's so much in there. This is a whole other podcast, really.
turns.zh: 嗯，我正试着把它整合起来，现在有了 AI，我更确信自己能把这些对话整合成有意义的东西。但对我来说，真正重要的是把未来 100 年扎根于我认为推动了这个国家建立的那些价值观：自由、平等、正义、生命、自由和追求幸福。然后把这些价值观应用到社会的不同领域。在教育领域，自由、平等和正义在实践中到底是什么样？那到底意味着什么？我们今天拥有的教育体系是 100 年前为美国的工业时代创建的。从上课铃到每个人学习做这些个性化的事情，再到获得 A、B、C 等级——所有这些都围绕着一个在今天美国并不常见的职业道路。如果我们必须根据我们的价值观重新思考教育体系，同时着眼于年轻人未来会做什么，我认为我们会为未来 100 年完全重新设计教育。这里面有太多东西了。这完全是另一个播客的话题。

turns.en: Is this really interesting or a documentary? Like, what is the format that you're creating?
turns.zh: 这真的很有趣，还是像纪录片？你正在创作的形式是什么样的？

turns.en: I'm trying to figure out what to turn it into, to be honest. It really just started out as a journey to learn and to begin to put the pieces together. My general concern is that as a society, we are not spending any time talking about our long-term success. In fact, if you look at all the ways our government makes decisions, almost all of them are inherently short-term. For example, the budget that Congress approves—our budget window is a 10-year budget window. What's really interesting is that any meaningful long-term investment, and the most obvious one is investments in young people, looks negative in 10 years. If you make an investment today in healthcare for young people, preschool for young people, food for young people, putting a roof over young people's head if they don't have one, over 30 years the returns on those investments are so profitable. There's no more profitable investment that our government could make than in young people. But when you look at those same investments over a 10-year time horizon, they look terrible—super negative. You're spending all this money to educate young people or to feed them or to provide them healthcare, and you're not yet seeing it show up in earnings later on. So we're just constantly making these terrible decisions where we distribute a huge amount of money to older folks in America, which is not necessarily the wrong thing to do. The problem is that we're just not distributing a lot of resources to young people. That means as a result, we're short-changing our future every single day. What really drew me to this was the realization that I'm just not hearing leaders talk about the next 100 years, what we need to do differently to set America up for success over a longer time horizon. Unfortunately, the government is best positioned to invest long-term. That's what's so surprising and frustrating about the whole thing. Who better to invest than the government, which is basically a legal monopoly in our country? They're so well positioned to invest long-term, but none of the incentives are set up around that.
turns.zh: 说实话，我正在琢磨把它变成什么形式。它最初只是一段学习之旅，开始把碎片拼凑起来。我主要的担忧是，作为一个社会，我们没有花任何时间来谈论我们的长期成功。事实上，如果你看看我们政府做决策的所有方式，几乎都是短视的。例如，国会批准的预算——我们的预算窗口是 10 年。真正有趣的是，任何有意义的长期投资，最明显的是对年轻人的投资，在 10 年内看起来都是负面的。如果你今天投资于年轻人的医疗保健、学前教育、食物、为无家可归的年轻人提供住所，30 年后这些投资的回报会非常丰厚。我们的政府没有比投资年轻人更有利可图的投资了。但当你在 10 年的时间跨度内看同样的投资时，它们看起来很糟糕——非常负面。你花了这么多钱来教育年轻人、养活他们或为他们提供医疗保健，但还没有看到这些投资在以后的收入中体现出来。所以我们一直在做这些糟糕的决定，把大量资金分配给美国的老年人，这不一定错。问题是我们没有把大量资源分配给年轻人。结果就是我们每天都在透支未来。真正吸引我的是，我意识到我没有听到领导人谈论未来 100 年，我们需要做哪些不同的事情来让美国在更长的时间跨度内取得成功。不幸的是，政府是最适合进行长期投资的。这正是整件事如此令人惊讶和沮丧的地方。还有谁比政府更适合投资呢？政府基本上是我们国家的合法垄断者。他们非常适合进行长期投资，但没有任何激励机制是围绕这一点建立的。

turns.en: Right, because the longest tenure you can have at the executive level is 8 years. So how do you reconcile that with having a multi-decade long goal?
turns.zh: 对，因为行政级别的最高任期是 8 年。那么你如何调和这一点与一个长达数十年的目标呢？

turns.en: Well, I think part of it is reframing the conversation and the measurement. I do think if Congress had the tools to look at their budget over 30 years, for example, it would show very clearly that investments in young people are very profitable. So part of it is just starting to shift that narrative and to create the tools to do that and to ask people, 'Yeah, okay, I see how your budget performs over 5 years or 10 years, but what does this mean actually over 30 years? And are there opportunities we're missing out on because we're just not looking at this over the appropriate time horizon.'
turns.zh: 嗯，我认为部分原因在于重新构建对话和衡量标准。我确实认为，如果国会有工具来审视他们 30 年的预算，例如，它会非常清楚地显示对年轻人的投资是非常有利可图的。所以部分原因就是开始转变这种说法，创造这样做的工具，然后问人们：‘好吧，我看到你的预算在 5 年或 10 年内的表现，但这在 30 年内实际上意味着什么？我们是否因为只是在适当的时间跨度内没有看到这一点而错过了机会？’

turns.en: What's another sector or industry that you would want to apply—what is it? Equality, justice, and freedom to—outside of education? What do you think is also really high importance?
turns.zh: 除了教育，你还想把这个——平等、正义和自由——应用到哪个领域或行业？你认为还有什么也非常重要？

turns.en: I think you could apply that frame to almost any part of America today, but one that would be right for that conversation, although in my learnings and research it seems like absolutely the hardest problem to tackle, is healthcare. If you look at the state budget here in California, healthcare is eating the state budget. And that's true in America as well. Healthcare costs have just absolutely exploded. Part of that's a good thing. If you look overall, our ability to provide really advanced care and create all this new technology, it doesn't surprise me that that's where some of the cost growth has been because it's important to be able to extend people's lives or have people live in healthier, more productive ways. But I think unless we're really able to innovate there, it's going to create a huge drag on our future as we reallocate more and more resources to healthcare. A lot of this ties back to—and I think this is very common today—a lot of times government is focused on where they're spending money. I don't think they're as focused on the cost side of that equation. Actually, the history of America would say that we've done an amazing job at productivity growth, at innovating to actually drive costs down and to make things more accessible to more people. That has really been one of the huge miracles of America's success over the last 250 years: we've made a lot of stuff that previously was really expensive and only accessible to a very small number of people really cheap and really widely available. As a result, everyone's quality of life in America has improved, but also around the world. So I think we need to apply that sort of innovator's mentality to a lot of these big problems. And healthcare seems like one that is absolutely intractable but is not going away as a huge issue for us to work on.
turns.zh: 我认为你可以把这个框架应用到今天美国的几乎任何领域，但有一个领域非常适合这种讨论，尽管在我的学习和研究中，它似乎是最难解决的问题，那就是医疗保健。如果你看看加州这里的州预算，医疗保健正在吞噬州预算。在美国也是如此。医疗成本已经完全爆炸了。部分原因是好事。从整体上看，我们提供真正先进的护理和创造所有这些新技术的能力，成本增长出现在那里并不让我惊讶，因为能够延长人们的生命或让人们以更健康、更高效的方式生活是很重要的。但我认为，除非我们真的能在那里创新，否则随着我们将越来越多的资源重新分配给医疗保健，它将对我们的未来造成巨大的拖累。这很大程度上又回到了——我认为这在今天很常见——很多时候政府关注的是他们把钱花在哪里。我不认为他们同样关注等式的成本方面。实际上，美国的历史表明，我们在生产力增长、通过创新实际降低成本、让更多人更容易获得产品方面做得非常出色。这确实是美国过去 250 年成功的巨大奇迹之一：我们把很多以前非常昂贵、只有少数人才能获得的东西变得非常便宜、非常普及。结果，美国每个人的生活质量都提高了，世界各地也是如此。所以我认为我们需要把这种创新者的心态应用到很多大问题上。医疗保健似乎是一个绝对棘手但不会消失的重大问题，需要我们努力解决。
